is an [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") that takes player input from its controls, processes it through electrical or computerized components, and displays output to an electronic monitor or similar display. All arcade video games are [coin-operated](/wiki/Coin-operated "Coin-operated") or accept other means of payment, housed in an [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet"), and located in [amusement arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") alongside other kinds of [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"). Until the early 2000s, arcade video games were the largest[[1]](#cite_note-vgmarket-1) and most technologically advanced[[2]](#cite_note-VF3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-thg-3) segment of the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry").

An **arcade video game** is an [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") that takes player input from its controls, processes it through electrical or computerized components, and displays output to an electronic monitor or similar display. All arcade video games are [coin-operated](/wiki/Coin-operated "Coin-operated") or accept other means of payment, housed in an [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet"), and located in [amusement arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") alongside other kinds of [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"). Until the early 2000s, arcade video games were the largest[[1]](#cite_note-vgmarket-1) and most technologically advanced[[2]](#cite_note-VF3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-thg-3) segment of the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry").

Early [prototypical](/wiki/Prototypical "Prototypical") entries *[Galaxy Game](/wiki/Galaxy_Game "Galaxy Game")* and *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")* in 1971 established the principle operations for arcade games, and [Atari](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.")'s *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* in 1972 is recognized as the first successful commercial arcade video game. Improvements in computer technology and gameplay design led to a [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games"), the exact dates of which are debated but range from the late 1970s to the early 1980s. This golden age includes *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")*, and *[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_(arcade_game) "Donkey Kong (arcade game)")*. The arcade industry had a resurgence from the early 1990s to mid-2000s, including *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")*, *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1992_video_game) "Mortal Kombat (1992 video game)")*, and *[Dance Dance Revolution](/wiki/Dance_Dance_Revolution "Dance Dance Revolution")*, but ultimately declined in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") as competing [home video game consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console") such as the [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") and [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox") increased in their graphics and gameplay capability and decreased in cost. Nevertheless, Japan, China, and South Korea continue to maintain a robust arcade industry in contemporary times.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Main article: [History of arcade video games](/wiki/History_of_arcade_video_games "History of arcade video games")

See also: [History of arcade games](/wiki/History_of_arcade_games "History of arcade games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Signed_Pong_Cabinet.jpg/250px-Signed_Pong_Cabinet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Signed_Pong_Cabinet.jpg)

*[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* is the first commercially successful arcade video game.

[Games of skill](/wiki/Game_of_skill "Game of skill") were popular [amusement-park](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park") [midway](/wiki/Midway_(fair) "Midway (fair)") attractions from the 19th century on. With the introduction of electricity and coin-operated machines, they facilitated a viable [business](/wiki/Industry_classification "Industry classification"). When [pinball](/wiki/Pinball "Pinball") machines with electric lights and displays were introduced in 1933 (but without the user-controller [flippers](/wiki/Flipper_(pinball) "Flipper (pinball)") which would not be invented until 1947) these machines were seen as [games of luck](/wiki/Game_of_luck "Game of luck"). Numerous states and cities treated them as amoral playthings for rebellious young people, and banned them into the 1960s and 1970s.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

[Electro-mechanical games](/wiki/Electro-mechanical_game "Electro-mechanical game") (EM games) appeared in [arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") in the mid-20th century. Following [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s EM game [*Periscope*](/wiki/Periscope_(arcade_game) "Periscope (arcade game)") (1966), the arcade industry experienced a "technological renaissance" driven by "audio-visual" EM novelty games, establishing the arcades as a suitable environment for the introduction of commercial video games in the early 1970s.[[6]](#cite_note-Smith-6) In the late 1960s, college student [Nolan Bushnell](/wiki/Nolan_Bushnell "Nolan Bushnell") had a part-time job at an arcade where he became familiar with EM games watching customers play and helping to maintain the machinery, while learning the game business.[[7]](#cite_note-NGen23-7)

The [early mainframe game](/wiki/Early_mainframe_game "Early mainframe game") *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962) inspired the first commercial arcade video game, *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")* (1971), created by Nolan Bushnell and [Ted Dabney](/wiki/Ted_Dabney "Ted Dabney") and released by [Nutting Associates](/wiki/Nutting_Associates "Nutting Associates").[[8]](#cite_note-verge_history-8) It was demonstrated at the Amusement & Music Operators Association (AMOA) show in October 1971.[[9]](#cite_note-TCW129135-9) Another *Spacewar*-inspired coin-operated video game, *[Galaxy Game](/wiki/Galaxy_Game "Galaxy Game")*, was demonstrated at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") in November 1971. Bushnell and Dabney followed their *Computer Space* success to create - with the help of [Allan Alcorn](/wiki/Allan_Alcorn "Allan Alcorn") - a table-tennis game, *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, released in 1972. *Pong* became a commercial success, leading numerous other coin-op manufacturers to enter the market.[[8]](#cite_note-verge_history-8)

Main article: [Golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games")

The video game industry transitioned from discrete integrated circuitry to programmable [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") in the mid-1970s, starting with *[Gun Fight](/wiki/Gun_Fight "Gun Fight")* in 1975. The arcade industry entered a "Golden Age" in 1978 with the release of [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito")'s *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, which introduced many novel [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay") features. From 1978 to 1982, several other major arcade-games from Namco, Atari, Williams Electronics, Stern Electronics, and Nintendo were all considered [blockbusters](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)"), particularly Namco's *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")* (1980), which became a fixture in [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"). Across North America and Japan, dedicated video-game arcades appeared and arcade-game cabinets appeared in many smaller storefronts. By 1981, the arcade video-game industry was worth US$8 billion in the US.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The novelty of arcade games waned sharply after 1982 due to several factors, including [market saturation](/wiki/Market_saturation "Market saturation") of arcades and arcade games and a [moral panic](/wiki/Moral_panic "Moral panic") over video games (similar to fears raised over pinball machines in the decades prior). The arcade market had recovered by 1986, with the help of software-conversion kits, the arrival of popular [beat 'em up](/wiki/Beat_%27em_up "Beat 'em up") games (such as [*Kung-Fu Master*](/wiki/Kung-Fu_Master_(video_game) "Kung-Fu Master (video game)") (1984) and [*Renegade*](/wiki/Renegade_(video_game) "Renegade (video game)") (1986–1987)), and advanced [motion simulator](/wiki/Motion_simulator "Motion simulator") games (such as Sega's "taikan" games including *[Hang-On](/wiki/Hang-On "Hang-On")* (1985), *[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")* (1985), and *[Out Run](/wiki/Out_Run "Out Run")* (1986)). However, the growth of home video-game systems such as the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") led to another brief arcade decline toward the end of the 1980s.[[11]](#cite_note-replaymag-11)

Arcade games continued to improve with the development of technology and of gameplay. In the early 1990s, the release of [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")* established the modern style of [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game") and led to a number of similar games such as *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat")*, *[Fatal Fury](/wiki/Fatal_Fury "Fatal Fury")*, *[Killer Instinct](/wiki/Killer_Instinct "Killer Instinct")*, *[Virtua Fighter](/wiki/Virtua_Fighter "Virtua Fighter")*, and *[Tekken](/wiki/Tekken "Tekken")*, creating a new renaissance in the arcades.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Another factor was realism,[[14]](#cite_note-GW-14) including the "3D Revolution" from [2D](/wiki/2D_graphics "2D graphics") and [pseudo-3D](/wiki/Pseudo-3D "Pseudo-3D") graphics to "true" [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_computer_graphics "Real-time computer graphics") [3D polygon graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics").[[15]](#cite_note-Williams-15)[[16]](#cite_note-tao2-16) This was largely driven by a technological [arms-race](/wiki/Arms_race "Arms race") between [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") and [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco").[[17]](#cite_note-RG127-17) During the early 1990s games such as Sega's *[Virtua Racing](/wiki/Virtua_Racing "Virtua Racing")* and *[Virtua Fighter](/wiki/Virtua_Fighter "Virtua Fighter")* popularized 3D-polygon technology in arcades. 3D graphics later became popular in console and computer games by the mid-1990s,[[18]](#cite_note-vracing-18) though arcade systems such as the [Sega Model 3](/wiki/Sega_Model_3 "Sega Model 3") remained considerably more advanced than home systems in the late 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-VF3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-thg-3) Until about 1996, arcade video-games had remained the largest segment of the global [video-game industry](/wiki/Video-game_industry "Video-game industry"). Arcades declined in the late 1990s, surpassed by the console market for the first time around 1997–1998.[[1]](#cite_note-vgmarket-1)

Since the 2000s, arcade games have taken different routes globally. In the United States, arcades have become niche markets as they compete with the home-console market, and they have adapted other business models, such as providing other entertainment options or adding prize redemptions.[[19]](#cite_note-socialarcades-19) In Japan, where arcades continue to flourish, games like *[Dance Dance Revolution](/wiki/Dance_Dance_Revolution "Dance Dance Revolution")* and *[The House of the Dead](/wiki/The_House_of_the_Dead "The House of the Dead")* aim to deliver tailored experiences that players cannot easily have at home.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Technology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Neogeoguts.JPG/250px-Neogeoguts.JPG)](/wiki/File:Neogeoguts.JPG)

The inside of a [Neo Geo MVS](/wiki/Neo_Geo "Neo Geo") arcade cabinet

Virtually all modern arcade games (other than the very traditional fair [midway](/wiki/Midway_(fair) "Midway (fair)")) make extensive use of solid state [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit"), and monitor screens, all installed inside an [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet").

With the exception of *Galaxy Game* and *Computer Space*, which were built around small form-factor [mainframe computers](/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer"), the first arcade games are based on combinations of multiple discrete logic chips, such as [transistor–transistor logic](/wiki/Transistor%E2%80%93transistor_logic "Transistor–transistor logic") (TTL) chips. Designing an arcade game was more about the combination of these TTL chips and other electronic components to achieve the desired effect on screen. More complex gameplay required significantly more TTL components to achieve this result. By the mid-1970s, the first inexpensive programmable [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessors "Microprocessors") had arrived on the market. The first microprocessor-based video game is Midway's *[Gun Fight](/wiki/Gun_Fight "Gun Fight")* in 1975 (a conversion of Taito's *Western Gun*), and with the advent of *Space Invaders* and the golden era, microprocessor-based games became typical.[[21]](#cite_note-Ultimate_History-21): 64 Early arcade games were also designed around [raster graphics](/wiki/Raster_graphics "Raster graphics") displayed on a [cathode-ray tube](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube") (CRT) display. Many games of the late 1970s and early 1980s use special displays that rendered [vector graphics](/wiki/Vector_graphics "Vector graphics"), though these waned by the mid-1980s as display technology on CRTs improved.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Prior to the availability of color CRT or vector displays, some arcade cabinets have a combination of angled monitor positioning, [one-way mirrors](/wiki/One-way_mirror "One-way mirror"), and clear overlays to simulate colors and other graphics onto the gameplay field.[[23]](#cite_note-arcade_design-23)

Coin-operated arcade video games from the 1990s to the 2000s generally use custom hardware often with multiple [CPUs](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit"), highly specialized [sound](/wiki/Sound_chip "Sound chip") and [graphics chips](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit"), and the latest in expensive [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") display technology. This allows more complex graphics and sound than contemporary [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") or [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer"). Many arcade games since the 2000s run on modified video game console hardware (such as the Sega NAOMI or Triforce) or gaming PC components (such as the [Taito Type X](/wiki/Taito_Type_X "Taito Type X")). Many arcade games have more immersive and realistic game controls than [PC](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") or [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game"). This includes specialized ambiance or control accessories such as fully enclosed dynamic cabinets with [force feedback](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology") controls, dedicated [lightguns](/wiki/Lightgun "Lightgun"), rear-projection displays, reproductions of automobile or airplane cockpits, motorcycle or horse-shaped controllers, or highly dedicated controllers such as [dancing mats](/wiki/Dance_pad "Dance pad") and [fishing rods](/wiki/Fishing_rod "Fishing rod"). These accessories are usually too bulky, expensive, and specialized to be used with typical home PCs and consoles. Arcade makers experiment with [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") technology. Arcades have progressed from using coins as credits to smart cards that hold the virtual currency of credits.

Modern arcade cabinets use flat panel displays instead of cathode-ray tubes. Internet services such as [ALL.Net](/wiki/ALL.Net "ALL.Net"), [NESiCAxLive](/wiki/NESiCAxLive "NESiCAxLive"), [e-Amusement](/wiki/E-Amusement "E-Amusement") and [NESYS](/wiki/NESYS "NESYS"), allow the cabinets to download updates or new games, do online multiplayer gameplay, save progress, unlock content, or earn credits.

## Genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Genres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Sega_Rally.jpg/250px-Sega_Rally.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sega_Rally.jpg)

*[Sega Rally](/wiki/Sega_Rally "Sega Rally")* arcade racing games at the [Veljekset Keskinen](/wiki/Veljekset_Keskinen "Veljekset Keskinen") department store in [Tuuri](/wiki/Tuuri "Tuuri"), [Alavus](/wiki/Alavus "Alavus"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") in 2017

Many arcade games have short levels, simple and intuitive control schemes, and rapidly increasing [difficulty](/wiki/Difficulty_level "Difficulty level"). The classic formula for a successful arcade video game is "easy to learn, difficult to master"[[24]](#cite_note-kunkel-24) along with a "multiple [life](/wiki/Life_(video_games) "Life (video games)"), progressively difficult [level](/wiki/Level_(video_games) "Level (video games)")" paradigm.[[25]](#cite_note-25) This is due to the environment of the arcade, where the player is essentially renting the game for as long as their in-game avatar can stay alive or until they run out of [tokens](/wiki/Token_coin "Token coin"). Games on consoles or PCs can be referred to as "arcade games" if they share these qualities, or are direct ports of arcade games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Arcade [racing games](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") often have sophisticated [motion simulator](/wiki/Motion_simulator "Motion simulator") [arcade cabinets](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet"),[[26]](#cite_note-arcadeheroes-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Horowitz2-27) a simplified [physics engine](/wiki/Physics_engine "Physics engine"), and short learning time when compared with more realistic [racing simulations](/wiki/Racing_simulation "Racing simulation"). Cars can turn sharply without braking or understeer, and the [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") rivals are sometimes programmed so they are always near the player with a [rubberband effect](/wiki/Rubberband_effect "Rubberband effect"). Other types of arcade-style games include [music games](/wiki/Music_game "Music game") (particularly [rhythm games](/wiki/Rhythm_game "Rhythm game")), and [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") and [casual games](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game") with intuitive controls and short sessions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Action

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Action")]

The term "arcade game" can refer to an [action video game](/wiki/Action_video_game "Action video game") designed to play similarly to an arcade game with frantic, addictive gameplay.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The focus of arcade action games is on the user's reflexes, and many feature very little puzzle-solving, complex thinking, or strategy skills.[[29]](#cite_note-Arcade-12/98-29) These include [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game") often played with an [arcade controller](/wiki/Arcade_controller "Arcade controller"), [beat 'em up](/wiki/Beat_%27em_up "Beat 'em up") games including fast-paced [hack and slash](/wiki/Hack_and_slash "Hack and slash") games, and [light gun](/wiki/Light_gun_shooter "Light gun shooter") [rail shooters](/wiki/Rail_shooters "Rail shooters") and "[bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell")" [shooters](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") with intuitive controls and rapidly increasing difficulty.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many arcade [combat flight simulation games](/wiki/Combat_flight_simulation_game "Combat flight simulation game") have sophisticated hydraulic motion simulator cabinets,[[26]](#cite_note-arcadeheroes-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Horowitz2-27) and simplified physics and handling. Arcade flight games are meant to have an easy [learning curve](/wiki/Learning_curve "Learning curve"), in order to preserve their action component. Increasing numbers of console flight video games, such as *[Crimson Skies](/wiki/Crimson_Skies:_High_Road_to_Revenge "Crimson Skies: High Road to Revenge")*, *[Ace Combat](/wiki/Ace_Combat "Ace Combat")*, and *[Secret Weapons Over Normandy](/wiki/Secret_Weapons_Over_Normandy "Secret Weapons Over Normandy")* indicate the falling of manual-heavy flight sim popularity in favor of instant arcade flight action.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

A modern subgenre of action games called "[hack and slash](/wiki/Hack_and_slash "Hack and slash")" or "character action games" represent an evolution of traditional arcade action games, and are sometimes considered a subgenre of beat 'em up brawlers. This subgenre of games was largely defined by [Hideki Kamiya](/wiki/Hideki_Kamiya "Hideki Kamiya"), creator of the *[Devil May Cry](/wiki/Devil_May_Cry "Devil May Cry")* and *[Bayonetta](/wiki/Bayonetta "Bayonetta")* franchises.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Industry")]

Arcade games are found in restaurants, bowling alleys, college campuses, video rental shops, dormitories, laundromats, movie theaters, supermarkets, shopping malls, airports, and other retail environments. They are popular in public places where people are likely to have free time.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Their profitability is expanded by the popularity of conversions of arcade games for home-based platforms. In 1997, [WMS Industries](/wiki/WMS_Industries "WMS Industries") (parent company of [Midway Games](/wiki/Midway_Games "Midway Games")) reported that if more than 5,000 arcade units are sold, at least 100,000 home version units will be sold.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The [American Amusement Machine Association](/wiki/American_Amusement_Machine_Association "American Amusement Machine Association") (AAMA) is a [trade association](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") established in 1981[[34]](#cite_note-34) that represents the American coin-operated amusement machine industry,[[35]](#cite_note-35) including 120 arcade game distributors and manufacturers.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The [Japan Amusement Machine and Marketing Association](/wiki/Japan_Amusement_Machine_and_Marketing_Association "Japan Amusement Machine and Marketing Association") (JAMMA) represents the Japanese arcade industry. Arcade machines may have standardized connectors or interfaces such as JAMMA, or JVS, that help with quick replacement of game systems or boards in arcade cabinets. The game boards or arcade boards may themselves allow for games to be replaced via game cartridges or discs.

## Conversions, emulators, and recreations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conversions, emulators, and recreations")]

Prior to the 2000s, successful video games were often [converted](/wiki/Video_game_conversion "Video game conversion") to a home video game console or home computer. Many of the initial [Atari VCS](/wiki/Atari_VCS "Atari VCS") games, for example, were conversions of Atari's success arcade games. Arcade game manufacturers that were not in the home console or computer business found licensing of their games to console manufacturers to be a successful business model, as console manufacturer competitors would vie for rights to more popular games. [Coleco](/wiki/Coleco "Coleco") famously bested Atari to secure the rights to convert Nintendo's *Donkey Kong*, which it subsequently included as a pack-in game for the [ColecoVision](/wiki/ColecoVision "ColecoVision") to challenge the VCS.[[37]](#cite_note-nl_bromley-37)

Arcade conversions typically had to make concessions for the lower computational power and capabilities of the home console, such as limited graphics or alterations in gameplay. Such conversions had mixed results. The Atari VCS conversion of *Space Invaders* was considered the VCS's [killer application](/wiki/Killer_application "Killer application"), helping to quadruple the VCS sales in 1980.[[38]](#cite_note-RG-41-38) In contrast, the VCS conversion of *Pac-Man* in 1982 was highly criticized for technical flaws due to VCS limitations such as flickering ghosts and simplified gameplay. Though *Pac-Man* was the best-selling game on the VCS, it eroded consumer confidence in Atari's games and partially contributed to the 1983 crash.[[39]](#cite_note-Gama-2600-39)

The need for arcade conversions began to wane as arcade game manufacturers like Nintendo, Sega, and SNK entered the home console market and used similar technology within their home consoles as found at the arcade, negating the need to simplify the game. Concessions still may be made for a home release; notably, the [Super Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Super_Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Super Nintendo Entertainment System") conversion of *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat_(1992_video_game) "Mortal Kombat (1992 video game)")* removed much of the gore from the arcade version to meet Nintendo's quality control standards.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Main article: [List of video game emulators § Arcade](/wiki/List_of_video_game_emulators#Arcade "List of video game emulators")

Exact copies of arcade video games can be run through [emulators](/wiki/Emulator "Emulator") such as [MAME](/wiki/MAME "MAME") on modern devices. An emulator is an application that translates foreign software onto a modern system, in real-time. Emulated games appeared legally and commercially on the [Macintosh](/wiki/Macintosh "Macintosh") in 1994[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) with Williams floppy disks, [Sony PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") in 1996, and [Sega Saturn](/wiki/Sega_Saturn "Sega Saturn") in 1997 with CD-ROM compilations such as *[Williams Arcade's Greatest Hits](/wiki/Williams_Arcade%27s_Greatest_Hits "Williams Arcade's Greatest Hits")* and *[Arcade's Greatest Hits: The Atari Collection 1](/wiki/Arcade%27s_Greatest_Hits:_The_Atari_Collection_1 "Arcade's Greatest Hits: The Atari Collection 1")*, and on the [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") and [GameCube](/wiki/GameCube "GameCube") with DVD-ROM compilations such as *[Midway Arcade Treasures](/wiki/Midway_Arcade_Treasures "Midway Arcade Treasures")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Arcade games are downloaded and emulated through the [Nintendo Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") [Virtual Console](/wiki/Virtual_Console "Virtual Console") service starting in 2009.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Using emulation, companies like [Arcade1Up](/wiki/Arcade1Up "Arcade1Up") have produced at-scale or reduced-scale recreations of arcade cabinets using modern technology, such as LCD monitors and lightweight construction. These cabinets are typically designed to resemble the original arcade game cabinets, but may also support multiple related games. These cabinets can be offered in diverse and miniaturized styles, such as table-mounted and wall-mounted versions.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Highest-grossing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arcade_video_game&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Highest-grossing")]

See also: [Golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games")

For arcade games, success is usually judged by either the number of [arcade hardware](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet") units sold to operators, or the amount of revenue generated. The revenue can include the coin drop earnings from coins (such as [quarters](/wiki/Quarter_(United_States_coin) "Quarter (United States coin)"), [dollars](/wiki/Australian_Dollar "Australian Dollar"), or [100 yen coins](/wiki/100_yen_coin "100 yen coin")) inserted into machines,[[44]](#cite_note-44) and/or the earnings from hardware sales with each unit costing thousands of dollars. Most of the revenue figures listed below are incomplete as they only include hardware sales revenue, due to a lack of available data for coin drop earnings which typically account for the majority of a hit arcade game's gross revenue. This list only includes arcade games that either sold more than 10,000 hardware units or generated a revenue of more than $10 million. Most of the games listed were released between the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") (1978–1984) and the 1990s.

This is a [dynamic list](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Lists#Dynamic_lists "Wikipedia:WikiProject Lists") and may never be able to satisfy particular standards for completeness.
